definition: A method should only call methods on objects it directly owns — not on objects returned by those methods.
ruleOfThumb: Count the dots in a method chain. Each extra dot is another object you're depending on that you don't own.

citation.author: David Thomas & Andrew Hunt
citation.bookTitle: The Pragmatic Programmer: Your Journey to Mastery

options.text: The method navigates a chain of objects to gather data — if any object in the chain changes its structure, this code breaks even though InvoiceService hasn't changed
options.explanation: InvoiceService depends not just on Order, but on Customer, Address, Account, and TaxExemption — objects it never directly owns. Renaming getRegion() or restructuring Account causes this method to break, even though none of those changes have anything to do with invoicing.

options.text: The method calls getCustomer() three separate times instead of caching the result in a variable
options.explanation: Caching customer = order.getCustomer() would reduce redundant calls, but the deeper problem remains: the method still navigates through Customer's internals. Even with a cached reference, it's still depending on objects it doesn't own.

options.text: TaxEngine is a static class, which makes the method impossible to test
options.explanation: Static dependencies are a testability concern, but they're not why this method is fragile. The chained navigation is the problem — restructuring any intermediate object in that chain silently breaks tax calculation.

options.text: Tax calculation logic doesn't belong in InvoiceService at all
options.explanation: InvoiceService doing tax calculation is reasonable. The issue isn't what it's doing — it's how deeply it reaches into Order's object graph to do it.

questions.prompt: A bug report says this code crashed when a user had no saved payment method. Which line is most likely responsible, and why?

options.text: The chained call — if getDefaultCard() returns null when no card exists, calling getLastFour() on it throws. The chain hides this possibility entirely.
options.explanation: Each step in the chain can return null or undefined, but the code treats every intermediate result as safe. The chain obscures the failure mode — there's no signal that 'user might have no payment profile' or 'profile might have no default card'.

options.text: getTotalPrice() might return 0 for an empty cart, which would cause a zero-amount charge
options.explanation: A zero-amount edge case is worth handling, but the bug report points to a crash — not a wrong amount. The null-safety gap in the payment chain is the likely culprit.

options.text: this.gateway.charge() should be awaited since payment processing is async
options.explanation: Async handling is a real concern for production payment code, but it would cause a type error or unhandled promise, not the specific crash described when a user has no payment method.

options.text: cardNumber should be validated to be exactly 4 characters before calling charge()
options.explanation: Validating the last-four digits is sensible, but the code never reaches that point when getDefaultCard() returns null. The chain fails before cardNumber is ever assigned.

questions.prompt: Both methods look up a shipping destination. Which version will break if the Customer model is refactored?

options.text: Version A — delegating to order.getShippingDestination() hides what data is actually used, making the method harder to debug
options.explanation: Hiding implementation details is the goal of encapsulation, not a drawback. If ShipmentService needs to debug a wrong city, it looks at Order.getShippingDestination() — one place, clearly owned by the right class.

options.text: Version B — it reaches directly into Order's customer, then into customer's address. If the Customer or Address structure changes, ShipmentService breaks even though shipping logic hasn't changed.
options.explanation: Version B knows the internal structure of both Order and Customer. Version A asks Order to format its own destination — Order is the only class that needs to know how customer and address are structured. Refactoring Customer only requires updating Order, not every class that used to navigate through it.
